results.title: Early 72-digit adjustment in sqrt will lead to incorrect result exponent calculation
results.reasoning: The described issue is a sqrt() bug in the 512-bit branch: performing the 72/73-digit mantissa adjustment before halving the exponent causes an off-by-one result exponent. The audit report’s sqrt findings cover different problems (sqrt(0) using stop, and an assessed-no-impact underflow in the Heron/else branch). No item addresses the ordering bug in the Uint512 sqrt path (rMan > MAX_L_DIGIT_NUMBER check before rExp /= 2) or proposes the corresponding fix. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: Square root function silently accepts invalid non-positive inputs
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit’s Issue #4 explicitly identifies that Float128.sqrt() uses the EVM stop() opcode when input is zero, halting execution without a revert. This is the same function, same code path, same underlying flaw, and the same suggested fix (return 0 instead of stop). Fixing the audit’s issue would fix the described vulnerability.

results.title: Natural logarithm function silently accepts invalid non-positive inputs
results.reasoning: Detected. The audit report contains two findings that together match the described issue: (1) Issue #2: ln() silently accepts negative inputs by masking out the sign bit and effectively computing ln(|x|) instead of reverting; (2) Issue #18: ln(0) returns a finite value instead of reverting. Both refer to the same code path (Ln.ln in Ln.sol) and the same root cause (missing domain checks for non-positive inputs). The specific remediation—adding checks to reject zero and negative inputs—would fix both the audit’s findings and the vulnerability description, so they are the same vulnerability.

results.title: Unwrapping while equating inside the eq function fails to account for the set L_MATISSA_FLAG
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit item #1 explicitly identifies that Float128.eq() performs a raw bitwise comparison (packedFloat.unwrap(a) == packedFloat.unwrap(b)), causing mathematically equal values encoded with different mantissa sizes (M vs L, due to the L_MANTISSA_FLAG) to compare unequal. This is the same function, same root cause, same code path, and the same fix (normalize/decode before comparing) would resolve both. Therefore, the audit detected the described vulnerability.

results.title: Precision loss in toPackedFloat function when mantissa is in range (MAX_M_DIGIT_NUMBER, MIN_L_DIGIT_NUMBER)
results.reasoning: The described issue is a precision-loss bug in toPackedFloat: the function decides M/L sizing based solely on the exponent and ignores digitsMantissa when the mantissa is between MAX_M_DIGIT_NUMBER and MIN_L_DIGIT_NUMBER, causing a downcast to M and loss of up to tens of digits. The audit report’s toPackedFloat findings (Items 9 and 17) concern unchecked exponent bounds and normalization-induced exponent overflow/underflow, not the M/L selection logic or precision downcast. No item mentions determining size solely from exponent, nor proposes incorporating digitsMantissa into isResultL. Fixing the audit’s issues (adding exponent range checks) would not fix the precision-loss downcast, and an attack exploiting the precision loss would not be addressed by those fixes. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.
